entries.label: Aristotle
entries.wd_description: 4th-century BCE Classical Greek philosopher and polymath

entries.label: Jeremy Bentham
entries.wd_description: British philosopher, jurist, and social reformer (1748–1832)

entries.label: Thomas Aquinas
entries.wd_description: Italian Dominican friar, philosopher, Catholic priest, and Doctor of the Church (1225–1274)

entries.label: Allan Gibbard
entries.wd_description: American philosopher and social choice theorist

entries.label: Normative Ethics
entries.wd_description: study of ethical action; branch of philosophical ethics that investigates questions about how one morally ought to act and examines standards for the rightness and wrongness of actions

entries.label: Metaethics
entries.wd_description: branch of ethics seeking to understand ethical properties

entries.label: Consequentialism
entries.wd_description: class of ethical theory basing standards of right & wrong on the consequences of actions

entries.label: Utilitarianism
entries.wd_description: ethical theory which holds that the right action is the one that maximizes aggregate well-being

entries.label: Rule Utilitarianism
entries.wd_description: form of utilitarianism that says an action is right as it conforms to a rule that leads to the greatest good

entries.label: Categorical Imperative
entries.wd_description: central concept in Kantian moral philosophy

entries.label: Hypothetical Imperative
entries.wd_description: Kantian philosophical concept; antonym of categorical imperative

entries.label: Duty
entries.wd_description: legal or moral requirement to take a certain course of action

entries.label: Kingdom of Ends
entries.wd_description: Kant’s thought experiment about a world in which all humans are treated as ends, not as means

entries.label: Autonomy
entries.wd_description: concept found in moral, political, and bioethical philosophy

entries.label: Moral Relativism
entries.wd_description: philosophical positions about the differences in moral judgments across peoples and cultures

entries.label: Moral Nihilism
entries.wd_description: meta-ethical view that nothing is intrinsically moral or immoral

entries.label: Moral Cognitivism
entries.wd_description: the meta-ethical view that ethical sentences express propositions and can therefore be true or false

entries.label: Non-Cognitivism
entries.wd_description: the meta-ethical view that ethical sentences do not express propositions and thus cannot be true or false

entries.label: Emotivism
entries.wd_description: metaethical view that moral statements are expressions of emotional attitudes rather than objective truths

entries.label: Expressivism
entries.wd_description: metaethical theory that moral sentences are not descriptive, but express an evaluative attitude toward an object

entries.label: Prescriptivism
entries.wd_description: metaethical view that ethical sentences function similarly to imperatives which are universalizable—whoever makes a moral judgment is committed to the same judgment in any situation where the same relevant facts obtain

entries.label: Moral Intuitionism
entries.wd_description: family of views in moral epistemology

entries.label: Ethical Egoism
entries.wd_description: ethical position that moral agents should act in their own self-interest

entries.label: Altruism
entries.wd_description: principle or practice of concern for the welfare of others

entries.label: Psychological Egoism
entries.wd_description: The view that true altruism in humans is impossible

entries.label: Hedonism
entries.wd_description: philosophy of pleasure as the highest value

entries.label: Eudaimonia
entries.wd_description: Aristotelian term for happiness or welfare

entries.label: Virtue
entries.wd_description: morally positive trait or quality deemed to be morally good

entries.label: Practical Wisdom
entries.wd_description: ancient Greek word for a type of wisdom or intelligence

entries.label: Akrasia
entries.wd_description: lack of self-control or state of acting against one's better judgment

entries.label: Practical Reason
entries.wd_description: the use of reason to decide how to act

entries.label: Moral Responsibility
entries.wd_description: status of morally deserving praise, blame, reward, or punishment for an act or omission, in accordance with one's moral obligations

entries.label: Free Will
entries.wd_description: ability of agents to be the ultimate source or originator of their choices, free from external determination

entries.label: Determinism
entries.wd_description: philosophical belief that all events are determined completely by previously existing causes

entries.label: Compatibilism
entries.wd_description: belief that free will and determinism are mutually compatible to one another

entries.label: Moral Dilemma
entries.wd_description: decision-making problem between two conflicting moral imperatives, neither of which is unambiguously acceptable or preferable

entries.label: Moral Status
entries.wd_description: description of intrinsic self-worth and dignity

entries.label: Supererogation
entries.wd_description: acts which are good but not morally required to be done

entries.label: Doctrine of Double Effect
entries.wd_description: set of ethical criteria permitting certain actions when one's otherwise legitimate act may also cause harm

entries.label: Divine Command Theory
entries.wd_description: theory that morality is commanded by, and originates only from, the divine

entries.label: Natural Law
entries.wd_description: system of law that is purportedly determined by nature, and is thus universal

entries.label: Is-Ought Problem
entries.wd_description: philosophical problem articulated by David Hume in 1739 about how one can deduce prescriptive statements (what ought to be) from descriptive statements (what is)

entries.label: Naturalistic Fallacy
entries.wd_description: ethical argument asserting that it is fallacious to explain something good reductively

entries.label: Open Question Argument
entries.wd_description: philosophical argument put forward by British philosopher G. E. Moore, to refute the equating of the property of goodness with some non-moral property, X, whether naturalistic (e.g. pleasure) or supernatural

entries.label: Well-Being
entries.wd_description: measure of how well life is to someone or a group with factors such as health, happiness and satisfaction

entries.label: Justice
entries.wd_description: broad idea of a situation where people receive that which they deserve

entries.label: Rights
entries.wd_description: fundamental legal, social, or ethical principles of freedom or entitlement according to some legal system, social convention, or ethical theory

entries.label: Stoicism
entries.wd_description: school of Hellenistic philosophy who held that the practice of virtue suffices to attain eudaimonia

entries.label: German Idealism
entries.wd_description: predominant philosophical movement in Germany around 1800

entries.label: Empiricism
entries.wd_description: theory that states that knowledge comes only or primarily from sensory experience

entries.label: Rationalism
entries.wd_description: philosophical view that reason should be the chief source of knowledge

entries.label: Scholasticism
entries.wd_description: method of critical thought which dominated teaching by the academics ("scholastics", or "schoolmen") of medieval universities in Europe from about 1100 to 1700

entries.label: Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals
entries.wd_label: Groundwork of the metaphysics of morals
entries.wd_description: Translation of Kant's Groundwork published in 1997

entries.label: The Metaphysics of Morals
entries.wd_description: work of political and moral philosophy by Immanuel Kant

entries.label: An Enquiry Concerning the Principles of Morals
entries.wd_label: An Enquiry Concerning the Principles of Morals

entries.label: An Introduction to the Principles of Morals and Legislation
entries.wd_label: An Introduction to the Principles of Morals and Legislation
entries.wd_description: intro to the Principles of Morals and Legislation

entries.label: On Liberty
entries.wd_description: non-fiction work by John Stuart Mill

entries.label: The Right and the Good
entries.wd_description: 1930 book by the Scottish philosopher David Ross

entries.label: Language, Truth and Logic
entries.wd_description: philosophical book by A. J. Ayer

entries.label: A Theory of Justice
entries.wd_description: work of political philosophy and ethics by John Rawls

entries.label: Leviathan
wd_aliases: Leviathan or The Matter, Forme and Power of a Commonwealth Ecclesiasticall and Civil

entries.label: What We Owe to Each Other
entries.wd_label: What We Owe to Each Other
